ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pizza food product class (FOODON_00002572); no specific Napoletana/Neapolitan pizza subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers pizza as a category; no specific Napoletana variant code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 covers pizza generically; no Napoletana-specific concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole Napoletana pizza, approximately 300 g, as typically served at a restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Pizza, cheese, with vegetables, no meat, regular crust — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 21299 adapted); anchovy-caper Neapolitan profile estimated from USDA Foundation Foods components: pizza dough, tomato sauce, anchovies, capers, mozzarella, olive oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry for Napoletana pizza with anchovies and capers exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pizza entries (cheese pizza, regular crust) and adjusted upward for sodium and protein to reflect anchovy and caper content, consistent with published Italian pizza composition data. Energy, macros, and sodium are cross-referenced against the Italian National Food Composition Database (CREA BDA) Neapolitan pizza profile (~250 kcal/100g, protein ~10g, fat ~9g, carbohydrate ~30g, sodium ~600mg).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; pizza, cheese, regular crust GI ~60
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Napoletana pizza with anchovies and capers. GI of 60 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cheese pizza on regular crust (GI ~60, medium), as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The anchovy and caper toppings do not materially alter the glycemic response, which is driven by the wheat-flour crust. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 90) / 100 = 54.0, based on 300 g serving containing approximately 90 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from anchovies, capers, cheese, and tomato sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Napoletana pizza consumption due to its high sodium content (approximately 620 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1860 mg per standard 300 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 60) and high glycemic load per serving (GL 54)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Napoletana pizza in small portions, as its medium GI of 60 and high glycemic load per standard serving (GL ~54) can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; pairing with a salad or non-starchy vegetables may help moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and sodium from anchovies and capers
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Napoletana pizza intake due to its saturated fat content (~3.5 g per 100 g from mozzarella) and high sodium load from anchovies and capers, both of which are associated with elevated cardiovascular risk when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Napoletana Pizza (Pizza Napoletana) is a classic Italian pizza originating from Naples, Italy. It is distinguished by its thin, hand-stretched wheat-flour dough base, topped with tomato sauce, anchovies, capers, mozzarella cheese, and a drizzle of olive oil, then baked at high temperature in a wood-fired or conventional oven. The result is a pizza with a slightly charred, chewy crust, a tangy tomato base, and a bold, savoury flavour profile from the salt-cured anchovies and brined capers. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 250 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 9 g fat (of which 3.5 g saturated), and 30 g carbohydrate, with notably high sodium (~620 mg/100 g) due to the anchovy and caper toppings. The glycemic index is approximately 60 (medium), driven by the refined wheat crust. Clinically, it is a high-sodium food of concern for individuals managing hypertension, and its medium-to-high glycemic load per serving warrants portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes. It contains gluten, fish (anchovies), and dairy (mozzarella), making it unsuitable for individuals with these allergies or intolerances. In Singapore, it is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore.
common_user_queries: What toppings are on a Napoletana pizza?
How many calories in a Napoletana pizza?
Is Napoletana pizza high in sodium?
What is the glycemic index of pizza?
What is the difference between Napoletana and Margherita pizza?

confidence_reason: No exact USDA or HPB entry for Napoletana pizza with anchovies and capers exists. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy pizza entries and cross-referenced against Italian national food composition data (CREA BDA). GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database, cheese pizza regular crust). Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source database for this pizza category. All other micronutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and CREA BDA Italian food composition data. Sodium is elevated due to anchovy and caper content and is a primary clinical concern. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cheese pizza, regular crust); no direct GI measurement for Napoletana pizza with anchovies and capers was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving = (60 × 90) / 100 = 54.0, based on 300 g serving with ~90 g carbohydrate. This record was sourced from field survey data (Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore - Boat Quay) and processed under Project Veritas v2. Italian cuisine does not require Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names under the multilingual rules; those fields are null by design. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy adapted values and are reported with medium confidence.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy adapted values cross-referenced with CREA BDA Italian food composition data, as no exact entry exists.
Adopted GI of 60 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry (cheese pizza, regular crust) per Atkinson et al. 2008.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 with explicit justification in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Applied correct GI category threshold: GI 60 is medium (56-69 range).
Set ontology match_type values to only allowed values: 'exact', 'closest_parent', or 'none'.
Confirmed Italian cuisine does not require Malay/Tamil/Hindi under G6 multilingual rules.
